data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== GitHub: agentic plan/execute split survey (Aider/Cline/OpenHands/etc.)
=== agent aaa8566d7c4f36417

All six research streams returned with strong primary-source grounding. I have comprehensive coverage. Let me synthesize the findings into the requested report.

---

# Plan-Verification Patterns in Coding Agents: Research Findings

A survey of how popular, actively-maintained coding-agent projects handle the planning→execution boundary, plan review/critique, and plan-approval gates — with a focus on your three special interests: (1) a **separate critic/reviewer agent** reviewing a plan before execution, (2) plans that must **name files/tests/dependencies** before acting, and (3) **probes/spikes/experiments at plan time**.

The headline finding up front, because it reframes your design space: **almost nobody ships an automated separate-agent plan-critic gate.** Across 18 projects, the planner/executor split is overwhelmingly *one agent loop with a swapped system prompt + restricted toolset*, and the enforced gate is *human approval*, not a reviewing model. Automated plan-critique exists only as wire-it-yourself framework patterns (LangGraph reflection/reflexion, AutoGen critic). The closest things to your "separate reviewer agent reviews the plan" idea are post-hoc evaluators (OpenHands critic, SWE-agent reviewer) that score *completed work*, not pre-execution plans. This is a genuine gap — which means rebar's plan-verification step would be a relatively novel mechanism, not a me-too feature. Build it deliberately.

---

## Per-project findings

Legend for the four questions: **(a)** planning a distinct phase? **(b)** planner a separate agent/model? **(c)** plan-review/critique or human-approval gate? **(d)** probes/experiments at plan time?

### Turnkey coding agents

**Aider** — https://github.com/Aider-AI/aider · ~46.4k★ · maintained but coasting (latest release v0.86.0 Aug 2025; recent commits are model-list housekeeping)
- (a) Conceptual only — architect+editor are two sequential model calls in one automated turn, not a user-facing phase.
- (b) **Yes, two separate models.** The *architect model* describes the solution in natural language; the *editor model* (`--editor-model`) translates that into structured file edits. This is the canonical generator/executor split you cited.
- (c) **No gate.** The architect's proposal flows straight into the editor — no human checkpoint, no reviewing model between them. Critical read: this split is about *competence* (pair a reasoning-strong model with an edit-reliable one), **not governance**. (Aider's general git auto-commit/shell-confirm safety nets are not architect-specific.)
- (d) No. The architect emits prose from existing context (repo map + added files); no exploration loop.

**Claude Code** — plan mode / ExitPlanMode · Anthropic, actively developed (docs reference v2.1.x, mid-2026)
- (a) **Yes, a first-class enforced permission-mode state** you enter (`Shift+Tab` / `/plan` / `--permission-mode plan`) and exit by approving.
- (b) **Same model, restricted to read-only** — not a separate planner agent. Write tools are withheld until approval. (The read-only guarantee is policy/prompt-enforced, not a hard sandbox.)
- (c) **Human-approved**, not agent-reviewed. ExitPlanMode surfaces approve/refine options; `Ctrl+G` lets you edit the plan yourself. The auto-mode action classifier is a *different* feature (reviews individual actions at exec time, not the plan). The one model-mediated path — Ultraplan browser review — is still human-gated.
- (d) **Read-only but not inert** — Claude *can run exploratory shell commands and read files* while planning; it just can't edit source. Notable nuance vs. Aider: "read-only" ≠ "no execution."

**OpenHands** (formerly OpenDevin) — https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands · ~77.7k★ · very active (release 1.8.0, Jun 2026). **Critical structural note: the agent logic moved to a separate SDK repo** (software-agent-sdk, ~827★); write-ups describing `agenthub/` classes describe the dead V0 monolith.
- (a) No — interleaved ReAct, no enforced plan-then-execute boundary. Planning survives only as discretionary in-loop tools (a TodoWrite-style `TaskTrackerTool`, an opt-in `PlanningFileEditorTool`); nothing gates on them.
- (b) No. CodeActAgent is the single ReAct executor. **The old `PlannerAgent` was removed (PR #5959, Jan 2025)** — a planner agent was tried and *deleted*. Delegation exists but is off by default and homogeneous (sub-agent-as-tool).
- (c) No plan-level gate. There's per-*action* confirmation (mid-loop, per-command) and a `critic/` that scores *completed* work for retry — a **post-hoc evaluator, not a pre-execution plan critic.**
- (d) No distinct de-risking stage; exploration is ordinary in-loop ReAct.

**SWE-agent** (Princeton) — https://github.com/SWE-agent/SWE-agent · ~19.6k★ · maintenance mode (v1.1.0 May 2025; team's active focus is mini-SWE-agent)
- (a) No — explicitly ReAct (thought+action interleaved), cited by name in the paper.
- (b) No — single agent, single model. `RetryAgent` is best-of-N of the same config, not planner+executor.
- (c) None at plan time. The `reviewer.py` machinery is **post-hoc best-of-N submission selection** (score finished trajectories 1–5), easy to mistake for plan critique but isn't one.
- (d) Emergent, not designed — trajectories happen to start with reproduce/localize, but it's interleaved, not an instrumented phase. The real engineering is the **ACI** (windowed viewer, search caps, edit-with-linter), not planning.
- *Contrast worth keeping:* **Agentless** (deliberately non-agentic fixed localize→repair→validate pipeline) and **AutoCodeRover** (bounded agency: a dedicated AST-aware retrieval phase separated from a patch phase) sit at the low-agency end of the spectrum.

**Cline** — https://github.com/cline/cline · ~63.5k★ · very actively maintained (multiple releases Jun 2026)
- (a) **Yes — explicit Plan ↔ Act toggle.** Plan explores/strategizes; Act executes. History carries across the switch.
- (b) **Different model per mode is first-class and configurable** ("Use different models for Plan and Act" — e.g. a strong reasoner to plan, a fast model to execute; auto-swaps on toggle). It's the same agent loop with swapped model + prompt. **Plan mode is genuinely no-edit/no-exec** (real tool gating).
- (c) Two gates: a *soft* mode-boundary (human flips the toggle when "aligned" — no enforced approval artifact) and the *hard, substantive* one — **per-action approval** of every edit/command during Act (relaxable via auto-approve).
- (d) **Yes — and the strongest named flow in the set:** `/deep-planning` runs (1) silent codebase investigation → (2) clarifying-question discussion → (3) generate an `implementation_plan.md` artifact → (4) spin up a tracked task. Exploration-grounded, produces a written plan.

**Roo Code** — https://github.com/RooCodeInc/Roo-Code · ~24.2k★ · **DISCONTINUED — repo archived May 15, 2026** (points users back to Cline; live descendant is Kilo Code)
- (a) Yes — multiple persona modes (Architect=plan, Code=execute, plus Ask/Debug/Orchestrator).
- (b) Per-mode *remembered* model (not a single dual-model toggle). **Finer-grained tool gating than Cline:** Architect mode = read + mcp + `edit` *regex-restricted to markdown only*. The **Orchestrator/Boomerang** mode delegates via `new_task` to isolated per-mode subtasks; results return via `attempt_completion`. (Delegation is per-mode, not per-model.)
- (c) No plan-approval gate; per-action approval during execution is the real gate.
- (d) Architect reads code while planning; no named deep-planning protocol like Cline's.

**Continue** — https://github.com/continuedev/continue · ~34.1k★ · very actively maintained (commits Jun 2026). **Most source-verifiable of the open agents.**
- (a) **Yes — a read-only-restricted Plan mode** (`"chat" | "agent" | "plan" | "background"`). The toolset is literally filtered to read-only built-in tools in source (`selectActiveTools.ts`). ⚠️ Caveat: the filter only covers built-in tools — **MCP tools pass through unfiltered**, relying on the prompt alone. (A real lesson for your design: tool-level enforcement beats prompt-level, and gaps matter.)
- (b) No — same loop, mode swaps toolset + system message.
- (c) Per-tool policies: **Ask First (default) / Automatic / Excluded**; edits and bash default to "ask."
- (d) Yes — read/grep/glob/semantic-search stay active in Plan mode.

**Cursor** (agent mode) — closed; cursor.com/docs · proprietary, no source/stars
- (a) **Yes — Plan Mode** (recent, ~v2.2): produces a **reviewable, editable Markdown plan** saved to `.cursor/plans/` with structured to-dos.
- (b) No — same Agent, different mode.
- (c) **Plan-approval gate** ("Click to build" — execution starts only on that click), plus a command gate. ⚠️ Default Run Mode is **Auto-review** (auto-runs allowlisted commands, file edits auto-apply); strict per-command approval requires opting into Allowlist mode. The marketing implies more friction than defaults deliver.
- (d) Yes — researches the codebase, asks clarifying questions before emitting the plan.

**Devin / Cognition** — closed; docs.devin.ai · proprietary, claims are doc/blog-sourced (not auditable)
- (a) **Yes — "Interactive Planning"** (named feature: build detailed plans before coding).
- (b) Not disclosed (described as a single agent with a planning *phase*).
- (c) **Soft/configurable approval** — "wait for my approval" button gated by an Agency setting, with a **30-second auto-proceed timeout** by default. The confidence-score (🟢🟡🔴) gating claim is **blog-only marketing**, absent from core docs.
- (d) Yes — "Initial Assessment" indexes the branch and searches for relevant files before proposing.

**GPT-Engineer** — https://github.com/gpt-engineer-org/gpt-engineer · ~55.2k★ but **ARCHIVED / EOL** (succeeded by lovable.dev; last push May 2025)
- (a) No — linear prompt-chaining; "planning" is a prompt directive inside one generation call.
- (b) No — single agent.
- (c) Opt-in `--clarify` Q&A loop before generating; always-on execute/apply-diff gates. No review of the generated source itself.
- (d) No — default generates a fresh project with zero exploration.

**GPT Pilot (Pythagora)** — https://github.com/Pythagora-io/gpt-pilot · ~33.7k★ but **STAGNANT** ("not being maintained anymore") and ⚠️ **had a supply-chain compromise** (malicious telemetry payload ~Aug 2025, patched Jun 2026 — rotate credentials if run from source in that window). Despite the health caveats, **architecturally the richest multi-role pipeline in the set** — worth studying as a design reference.
- (a) **Yes, multi-stage**, enforced by an **Orchestrator state machine** (not the agents): Wizard → SpecWriter → Architect → TechLead (breaks epic into a `DevelopmentPlan` of `Task`s) → Developer (decomposes a task into concrete steps) → CodeMonkey/Executor → Troubleshooter/BugHunter.
- (b) **Yes — genuinely separate planner roles** (Architect + TechLead produce the plan; Developer decomposes each task into a structured `TaskSteps` list). ⚠️ The README's "Reviewer"/"Product Owner"/"Debugger" agents have no source files — they map onto other agents.
- (c) **Many hard human gates** (the strongest of the set): approve spec → confirm architecture/deps → **review task breakdown** ("Are you happy with the breakdown?") → approve/edit/skip each task → per-step intervention → an LLM code-review inside CodeMonkey → test the running app.
- (d) **Yes — and the only one that probes the *environment*, not just the repo:** SpecWriter clarifies, a relevant-files mixin identifies files before coding, and **the Architect actually runs each dependency's `test` command to probe the host environment.** This is the closest thing in the survey to a plan-time spike.

### Frameworks (planning/critique primitives)

**LangGraph** — https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph · ~35.2k★ · active (Jun 2026)
- (a)/(b) Plan-and-execute (`planner → agent → replan` graph, separate node/model/schema) is a **tutorial pattern, not a built-in**.
- (c) **Reflection / Reflexion (separate generator + critic loop) are tutorial recipes, not shipped classes** — Reflexion (arXiv 2303.11366) is notable: a structured `Reflection` schema with `missing`/`superfluous` fields, grounded by external search between draft and revise. The *shipped* primitive is human-in-the-loop: `interrupt()` + `Command(resume=...)` (approve/reject/edit state) — the enforced gate is whatever you build on it.
- (d) None — the planner is a single static structured-output call.

**AutoGen** — https://github.com/microsoft/autogen · ~59.1k★ · ⚠️ ~2 months stale (last push Apr 2026; converging into the Microsoft Agent Framework)
- (a)/(b) **The one genuinely shipped planner: Magentic-One's Orchestrator** — an outer planning loop builds a **Task Ledger** (facts + plan) before an inner execution loop runs against a **Progress Ledger**; re-plans on stall via `max_stalls`. Orchestrator plans/tracks and never executes; WebSurfer/FileSurfer/Coder execute.
- (c) **No canonical built-in Critic class** — assistant+critic reflection is always two ordinary agents (composable via `register_nested_chats` / `SocietyOfMindAgent`). The only built-in gate is human approval (`UserProxyAgent` / `human_input_mode` / `approval_func`). Magentic-One re-plans on stalls but never submits its plan to a separate critic before executing.
- (d) **Yes — the best plan-time epistemics in the set:** the Task Ledger prompt explicitly separates `GIVEN/VERIFIED FACTS` vs `FACTS TO LOOK UP` vs `FACTS TO DERIVE` vs `EDUCATED GUESSES` *before committing the plan*. It's a to-do list of probes, not speculative execution — but it's a structured way to force a plan to declare its unknowns.

**CrewAI** — https://github.com/crewAIInc/crewAI · ~53.9k★ · active (Jun 2026)
- (a)/(b) **Shipped, real:** `Crew(planning=True)` runs a dedicated planner `Agent` ("Task Execution Planner") with its own `planning_llm` once before any task, producing a typed `PlanPerTask` per task — but it's then **string-concatenated onto each task's description** (becomes prompt text, no separate plan object).
- (c) **No gate over the plan itself.** Review mechanisms gate *outputs*: a hierarchical manager agent reviews worker output; **task guardrails** are a genuine validate→reject→retry loop (`guardrail_max_retries`); `human_input=True` gates the final answer. No dedicated critic primitive.
- (d) None — the planner has no tools; it's one static call over a stringified task summary.

**MetaGPT** — https://github.com/FoundationAgents/MetaGPT · ~68.9k★ · maintenance/low-activity (pivoted commercially to atoms.dev; core pipeline work stopped ~mid-2025)
- (a) Real artifact pipeline (PRD → design → tasks → code) but **no explicit phase boundary in code** — ordering emerges from message subscriptions, no controller/gate.
- (b) Separate `Role` instances with distinct prompts, but **typically the same underlying LLM** (model-separation is supported, not default). The "assembly line" is a shared message pool, not direct calls.
- (c) ⚠️ **The most over-marketed review story in the survey.** Review *action classes exist but are dead/unwired code*: `DesignReview`, `WritePRDReview`, `WriteReview` (which even has an explicit `LGTM`/`LBTM` verdict) are imported only by tests — **no role ever calls them.** The only review that runs is post-plan, code-level `WriteCodeReview` — opt-in, advisory, and it never blocks (returns code regardless of verdict after k iterations). There is no Architect-reviews-PRD gate.
- (d) No — planning roles only call the LLM to emit text; executable feedback is QA-stage only.

**Plandex** — https://github.com/plandex-ai/plandex · ~15.5k★ · ⚠️ low-activity/wind-down (main dormant ~8 months; Plandex Cloud wound down Oct 2025)
- (a) Partial — plans are first-class objects with a **sandbox of pending diffs**, but the tentative state is the *file write to disk*, not a pre-approved plan (the model has already planned and coded).
- (b) **Yes — genuine model-level role separation:** 9 roles incl. distinct `planner` vs `coder` vs `architect` vs `builder`. ⚠️ But "Opus plans, Sonnet codes" is a *non-default* pack; the shipped default shares Sonnet across planner/coder and downshifts only mechanical roles to cheap models. **No `verifier` role exists.**
- (c) **Human diff-review** before apply (`diff` → `apply`/`reject`); no automated semantic gate; **bypassed entirely at full autonomy.**
- (d) Real but post-apply: `debug` repeatedly runs a command and fixes until it passes — an *apply→run→debug* self-heal loop **after** changes land, not a probe that informs the plan.

---

## Ranked: the most reusable, battle-tested patterns for a separate-agent plan-review gate

Ranked by how proven *and* how directly reusable for your specific goal (a reviewer agent critiquing a plan before execution). I've been honest about which are "proven in production" vs. "proven as a pattern."

**1. Read-only planner with hard tool-gating, not prompt-gating — proven by Continue (and Cline).**
The dominant production design isn't a separate agent; it's *one model in a mode where write/exec tools are physically removed from the toolset*. Continue implements this as a literal tool filter in source; Cline makes Plan mode no-edit/no-exec. The reusable lesson for rebar: **enforce your reviewer's read-only/no-side-effect constraint at the tool layer, not in the prompt** — and mind Continue's documented gap (MCP tools slipped past the built-in filter). If your reviewer agent runs probes, gate exactly what it can touch. This is the single most battle-tested primitive here.

**2. Separate planner role that emits a structured, decomposed plan artifact — proven by GPT Pilot (Architect/TechLead → `DevelopmentPlan`/`Task`s) and CrewAI (`planning=True` → typed `PlanPerTask`).**
If you want a plan that *names files/tests/dependencies before acting* (your interest #2), the proven move is a dedicated planner role that outputs a **typed/structured plan object** (not free prose), which a downstream step — your reviewer — can mechanically check field-by-field. GPT Pilot's TechLead-decomposes-epic-into-tasks and CrewAI's Pydantic `PlanPerTask` are the two shipped exemplars. rebar already has the substrate for this (acceptance-criteria blocks, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`) — a structured plan that *must* populate those is directly in line with this pattern, and would make a reviewer's job checkable rather than vibes-based.

**3. Generator + separate critic loop with a structured critique schema — proven as a pattern by LangGraph Reflexion and AutoGen reflection (and as the discriminating case AGAINST MetaGPT).**
This is the closest match to your core goal, and the honest finding is **no turnkey coding agent ships it — it exists only as framework patterns.** The reusable design: a *separate* critic model, prompted as an adversarial reviewer, emits a **structured verdict** (LangGraph Reflexion's `missing`/`superfluous` fields; a `LGTM`/`LBTM`-style verdict) that the planner must address before the plan is accepted. MetaGPT is the cautionary tale: it *wrote* exactly these classes (`WriteReview` with LGTM/LBTM) and **never wired them in** — so the lesson is that a critic only counts if it's on the enforced path and its verdict actually blocks. Your rebar context already has the better building blocks here: the **completion-verifier agent** (PASS/FAIL with per-finding source citations) is precisely a structured-critique-with-verdict mechanism — applying that same shape to *plans* (not just completions) is the natural, defensible extension, and would put rebar ahead of every project surveyed.

**4. Pre-plan epistemic ledger that forces the plan to declare unknowns — proven by AutoGen Magentic-One's Task Ledger.**
Before committing a plan, force it to enumerate `VERIFIED FACTS` vs `FACTS TO LOOK UP` vs `FACTS TO DERIVE` vs `EDUCATED GUESSES`. This is the most reusable *de-risking* pattern (your interest #3 without needing to execute code): a reviewer agent can specifically attack the "educated guesses" and "facts to look up" — the exact places a plan is most likely wrong. Cheap, model-only, no sandbox required, and it makes plan risk legible to a critic.

**5. Plan-time environment probe (a real spike), not just a repo read — proven (narrowly) by GPT Pilot's Architect running dependency `test` commands; supported by Claude Code/Cline/Cursor read-and-explore planning.**
True plan-time *experiments* are rare. GPT Pilot's Architect actually executing each dependency's test command to probe the host is the only genuine environment-spike in the survey. Most others (Claude Code, Cline `/deep-planning`, Cursor, Devin) probe by *reading/searching* the codebase, which de-risks file/symbol assumptions but not runtime ones. If you want rebar's planner/reviewer to run a real spike, GPT Pilot is your precedent — and pattern #1 (hard tool-gating) is the prerequisite that makes letting a planner execute *safe*.

### The strategic takeaway for your design
Your instinct is sound and the space is open: **a separate reviewer agent that critiques a plan before execution is, today, essentially unbuilt in shipping coding agents** — the field stops at human-approval gates (Claude Code, Cline, Cursor, Devin, GPT Pilot) and post-hoc work-scoring critics (OpenHands, SWE-agent). The reusable ingredients all exist but are scattered: read-only tool-gating (Continue/Cline), structured plan artifacts (GPT Pilot/CrewAI), structured critique verdicts (LangGraph Reflexion / rebar's own completion-verifier), and epistemic ledgers (Magentic-One). rebar is unusually well-positioned to combine them because it already has the structured-ticket substrate (`set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`, AC blocks) and a HMAC-signed PASS/FAIL verifier agent — turning that verifier on *plans* rather than only *completions* is the highest-leverage, most-defensible version of what you're proposing.

A repeated caveat to carry forward: **distinguish shipped-and-enforced from written-but-dead and tutorial-only.** MetaGPT's reviewers are dead code; LangGraph/AutoGen critics are patterns you assemble; CrewAI/AutoGen-Magentic-One/GPT-Pilot planners are the only genuinely shipped separate planners. Design rebar's gate so the verdict is *on the enforced path and actually blocks* — that's the line MetaGPT crossed on paper but not in code.
